def: A formulation that releases its full dose right after intake — quick onset but shorter, less steady coverage than extended-release.
A formulation that releases drug slowly for once-daily, all-day coverage with smoother levels and fewer peaks and troughs.
An abnormally slow heart rate, under about 60 beats per minute.
Excessive pupil constriction (pinpoint pupils) — a classic sign of opioid or cholinergic effect.
Pupil dilation — seen in sympathomimetic (e.g. stimulant) and anticholinergic toxicity.
An acute dystonic reaction with sustained forced upward deviation of the eyes; reverse with an IM/IV anticholinergic.
Sustained twisting or tilting of the neck; an acute drug-induced dystonia is one cause and responds to anticholinergics.
Sudden sustained muscle spasms (neck, eyes, tongue, back) soon after a dopamine blocker; treat with an IM/IV anticholinergic.
Antipsychotic-caused tremor, rigidity and slowed movement mimicking Parkinson disease, from D2 blockade in the striatum.
A distressing inner restlessness with an urge to move; a common antipsychotic effect, best treated with a beta-blocker.
A drop in blood pressure on standing, causing dizziness or fainting; often from alpha-1 blockade by psych drugs.
A transient loss of consciousness from a brief fall in blood flow to the brain (fainting).
A prolonged, often painful erection unrelated to arousal; a urologic emergency linked to alpha-1 blockers like trazodone.
Inappropriate milk discharge from the breast, driven by high prolactin (e.g. from D2-blocking antipsychotics).
Enlargement of male breast tissue; here from antipsychotic-induced high prolactin.
Absence of menstrual periods; a sign of high prolactin from dopamine-blocking drugs.
Elevated blood prolactin from D2 blockade; causes galactorrhea, menstrual changes, gynecomastia and sexual dysfunction.
Profuse sweating; a feature of serotonin syndrome, withdrawal, and sympathetic overactivity.
Exaggerated deep-tendon reflexes; with clonus, a hallmark of serotonin syndrome (worse in the legs).
Rhythmic involuntary muscle contractions on stretch; inducible or spontaneous clonus is a key serotonin-syndrome sign.
Rhythmic involuntary shaking; a fine tremor is common with lithium/SSRIs, while a coarse tremor suggests lithium toxicity.
Impaired coordination and unsteady gait; a warning sign of lithium toxicity.
Stiff, increased muscle tone; 'lead-pipe' rigidity with fever is a cardinal sign of neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Breakdown of skeletal muscle releasing myoglobin and CK, risking acute kidney injury; a complication of NMS.
Visible slowing of movement, speech and thought, often seen in severe depression.
Restless, purposeless motor activity from inner tension; seen in agitation, mania and mixed states, and a behavioural emergency.
Transient reddening and warmth of the skin; a feature of the disulfiram-alcohol reaction.
Passing abnormally large volumes of urine; classically from lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus or high glucose.
Excessive thirst and drinking, often paired with polyuria in diabetes insipidus or hyperglycemia.
Abnormal tingling or 'pins and needles'; a common topiramate effect from carbonic-anhydrase inhibition.
Sudden, brief, involuntary muscle jerks; can appear in serotonin syndrome.
Inability to empty the bladder; an anticholinergic effect, worse in men with prostate enlargement.
Numbness of the mouth or tongue; an expected local effect of sublingual asenapine.
Slowed thinking and trouble finding words — a recognized topiramate cognitive effect.
A perception without an external stimulus — e.g. hearing voices (auditory) or seeing things (visual).
A fixed false belief held firmly despite clear contrary evidence.
The 'taken-away' features of schizophrenia — blunted emotion, reduced speech, social withdrawal and loss of drive.
The 'added-on' features of psychosis — hallucinations, delusions and disorganized thought.
An early phase of subtle symptoms and gradual functional decline before a disorder fully declares itself.
A distinct period of abnormally elevated or irritable mood with high energy, reduced sleep and impaired judgment; defines bipolar I.
A milder, shorter mania without psychosis or major functional impairment; the mood high that defines bipolar II.
Loss of contact with reality — hallucinations, delusions and disorganized thinking.
Fear and avoidance of situations where escape feels hard or help unavailable; often develops after panic attacks.
A chronic disorder of excessive daytime sleepiness with sudden sleep attacks; modafinil is first-line.
Repeated upper-airway collapse during sleep causing apneas and daytime sleepiness; treated with CPAP.
Persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep despite adequate opportunity, with daytime impairment.
An acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness from a medical cause; the hypoactive form is easily missed.
Persistent, excessive worry about everyday things for 6+ months, with restlessness, tension and poor sleep.
A sudden surge of intense fear peaking within minutes (palpitations, breathlessness, doom); recurrent attacks plus dread define panic disorder.
Intense fear of scrutiny or embarrassment in social situations, leading to avoidance.
Intrusive, unwanted obsessions and repetitive compulsions done to relieve anxiety; treated with SSRIs and exposure therapy (ERP).
Intrusive memories, nightmares, avoidance and hyperarousal persisting after a traumatic event.
An episode of 2+ weeks of low mood or anhedonia plus changes in sleep, appetite, energy, concentration and self-worth.
A mood disorder alternating manic/hypomanic highs with depressive lows; antidepressant monotherapy is avoided.
A chronic psychotic disorder with positive (hallucinations, delusions), negative (withdrawal, flat affect) and cognitive features.
Hallucinations or delusions in Parkinson disease; treated with low-D2 agents (quetiapine, pimavanserin) that spare movement.
A progressive decline in memory and other cognition severe enough to impair daily function; Alzheimer is the commonest cause.
Problematic alcohol use with tolerance, withdrawal and loss of control; treated with naltrexone, acamprosate or disulfiram plus support.
The syndrome after stopping heavy drinking — tremor, anxiety, autonomic arousal, seizures and delirium tremens; treated with benzodiazepines.
Compulsive opioid use with tolerance and withdrawal; opioid agonist therapy is first-line and reduces mortality.
The syndrome after stopping opioids — dysphoria, sweating, GI upset, yawning, dilated pupils; distressing but rarely life-threatening.
Treating opioid use disorder with a long-acting opioid (buprenorphine or methadone) to stop withdrawal and cut overdose deaths.
Nicotine dependence; first-line quit aids are varenicline, nicotine replacement and bupropion.
Inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity beginning before age 12, present in 2+ settings and impairing function.
A mood or anxiety disorder in pregnancy or the year after birth; treatment balances maternal illness against fetal/infant exposure.
Depression that hasn't responded to adequate trials of two or more antidepressants; where MAOIs and augmentation are considered.
Schizophrenia that fails two adequate antipsychotic trials; clozapine is the proven next step.
A person's initial episode of psychotic illness; early referral to specialized services improves outcomes.
Nerve damage from diabetes causing burning, painful feet/hands; treated with duloxetine, TCAs or pregabalin.
A potentially fatal excess of serotonin — agitation, autonomic instability, clonus and hyperreflexia (legs>arms), hyperthermia.
A rare, life-threatening reaction to dopamine blockers — fever, lead-pipe rigidity, autonomic instability and high CK over days.
Drug-induced movement disorders from D2 blockade — acute dystonia, parkinsonism, akathisia and, later, tardive dyskinesia.
Late, often irreversible involuntary movements (classically orofacial) after prolonged D2 blockade; treat with a VMAT2 inhibitor, not anticholinergics.
The antimuscarinic toxidrome — 'hot, dry, red, blind, mad': fever, dry flushed skin, urinary retention, blurred vision, delirium.
Toxicity from a high lithium level — coarse tremor, ataxia, slurred speech, confusion; precipitated by dehydration, NSAIDs, thiazides, ACE inhibitors.
The cluster of weight gain, high glucose and dyslipidemia many antipsychotics cause (esp. olanzapine, clozapine); monitor regularly.
Flu-like symptoms, dizziness and 'brain zaps' after abruptly stopping an antidepressant; worst with short-half-life agents (paroxetine, venlafaxine).
A hypertensive crisis when tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, cured meats, draught beer) are eaten on an irreversible MAOI.
Lengthening of the ECG QT interval, raising the risk of torsades de pointes; a concern with ziprasidone and IV haloperidol.
A polymorphic ventricular tachycardia arising from a prolonged QT interval; can be fatal.
A dangerous fall in neutrophils (ANC under ~500) leaving the patient prone to serious infection; the key clozapine risk.
A low neutrophil count, raising infection risk; tracked closely on clozapine via the ANC.
A low blood sodium level; a recognized effect of carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine and SSRIs.
A life-threatening blistering reaction of skin and mucosa; a slow-titration concern for lamotrigine and carbamazepine.

aliases: drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms
def: A severe multi-organ drug hypersensitivity with rash, fever and eosinophilia (e.g. carbamazepine, linked to HLA-A*3101).
Drug-induced liver injury, from enzyme rises to fatal failure; a boxed-warning risk with valproate, a caution with duloxetine.
Inflammation of the pancreas causing severe abdominal pain; a recognized valproate adverse effect.
A low platelet count raising bleeding risk; can occur with valproate.
Confusion or lethargy from a high blood ammonia level, which valproate can cause even with normal liver enzymes.
Inflammation of the heart muscle; an early (first 6-8 weeks) clozapine risk — watch for chest pain, fever and breathlessness.
A hyperglycemic emergency with ketones and acidosis; olanzapine and clozapine can precipitate new diabetes and DKA.
Abnormal blood lipids (high LDL/triglycerides); part of antipsychotic metabolic syndrome.
A dangerously raised body temperature; a cardinal feature of NMS, serotonin syndrome and stimulant toxicity.
Fluctuating blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and sweating from autonomic dysregulation; a feature of NMS and serotonin syndrome.
A sharp blood-pressure surge after abruptly stopping an alpha-2 agonist (clonidine, guanfacine) or beta-blocker; always taper.
A transient worsening of sleeplessness after stopping a hypnotic, worse with short-acting agents.
Compulsive gambling, shopping, hypersexuality or binge-eating triggered by dopamine agonists/partial agonists; ask directly.
Kidney insensitivity to ADH causing dilute polyuria and thirst; a classic chronic lithium effect.
An underactive thyroid (fatigue, weight gain, low mood, constipation); a chronic lithium effect — monitor TSH.
A high blood calcium level; chronic lithium can raise PTH and calcium (hyperparathyroidism).
A sudden rise in eye pressure that threatens vision; anticholinergics and topiramate can precipitate it.
Neonatal hypotonia, sedation and poor feeding after late-pregnancy benzodiazepine exposure.
Marked orthostatic hypotension or syncope after the first dose of an alpha-1 blocker like prazosin; start low at bedtime.
Abrupt, severe opioid withdrawal when a high-affinity partial agonist (buprenorphine) displaces a full agonist; induce only once in objective withdrawal.
A substance that causes fetal malformation; valproate is the worst mood stabilizer (neural-tube defects, lower childhood IQ).
A serious birth defect of the brain or spine (e.g. spina bifida) from faulty neural-tube closure; risk raised by valproate/carbamazepine.
A cleft of the lip or palate; topiramate in pregnancy raises this risk.
The time for a drug's blood level to fall by half; it drives dosing frequency, accumulation and how fast a drug clears.
Gradually adjusting a drug's dose up or down to balance effect against tolerability.
An inactive drug the body converts into its active form (e.g. lisdexamfetamine), which can lower misuse potential.
A breakdown product of a drug that is itself pharmacologically active (e.g. desvenlafaxine from venlafaxine).
Extensive gut/liver metabolism of an oral drug before it reaches the blood, cutting bioavailability; bypassed by sublingual routes.
The fraction of an administered dose that reaches the bloodstream in active form.
A liver enzyme that metabolizes many antidepressants and antipsychotics; inhibitors (paroxetine, fluoxetine) raise substrate levels.
A major drug-metabolizing enzyme; inducers (carbamazepine, modafinil) lower substrate levels and inhibitors (grapefruit) raise them.
A liver enzyme induced by cigarette smoke; starting or stopping smoking shifts clozapine and olanzapine levels.
The point where drug intake and elimination balance out, giving stable levels — reached after about 5 half-lives.
A drug-free interval when switching medications to avoid dangerous interactions (e.g. 14 days off an MAOI before an SSRI).
A larger initial dose used to reach therapeutic levels quickly (e.g. valproate loading in acute mania).
A drug that binds a receptor but activates it only weakly — behaving as an agonist where signaling is low and a blocker where it is high.
A drug that fully activates a receptor; at the mu-opioid receptor this means no ceiling on respiratory depression (e.g. methadone).
A drug that binds a receptor and produces the opposite effect to an agonist; pimavanserin is a 5-HT2A inverse agonist.
The margin between an effective dose and a toxic one; lithium's is narrow, so levels and toxicity are a constant concern.
Measuring blood drug levels to guide dosing; useful for lithium, clozapine and nortriptyline.
The blood-level range where a drug works without toxicity; nortriptyline is unusual in that levels above it lose efficacy.
One of two mirror-image forms of a molecule; escitalopram and dexamphetamine are marketed as single active enantiomers.
A liver conjugation step (not oxidative CYP) that clears the 'LOT' benzos — lorazepam, oxazepam, temazepam — safer in liver disease.
When a drug speeds up its own metabolism, lowering its level over the first weeks; carbamazepine is the classic example.
A drug that boosts metabolizing enzymes, lowering co-drug levels — carbamazepine can cause oral-contraceptive failure this way.
A drug that blocks metabolizing enzymes, raising co-drug levels (e.g. valproate slowing lamotrigine's clearance).
The strongest safety warning a regulator can require on a drug label, flagging serious or life-threatening risks.
A long-acting antipsychotic injection releasing slowly over weeks, improving adherence for patients who can't take daily pills.
A steroid that modulates brain receptors; brexanolone (allopregnanolone) is a GABA-A neurosteroid for postpartum depression.
A drug binding a site distinct from the main one to enhance a receptor's response (e.g. benzodiazepines at GABA-A).
Given under the tongue to absorb directly into blood, bypassing first-pass metabolism (e.g. asenapine, buprenorphine).
A substance mimicking sympathetic 'fight-or-flight' activity — raising heart rate and blood pressure (e.g. amphetamines, decongestants).
A drug that stimulates dopamine receptors; can trigger impulse-control disorders like gambling and hypersexuality.
A drug (clonidine, guanfacine) that reduces sympathetic outflow; used in ADHD but needs tapering to avoid rebound hypertension.
A drug blocking beta-adrenergic receptors; propranolol is first-line for antipsychotic-induced akathisia and performance anxiety.
A drug blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors; relieves acute dystonia/parkinsonism but causes dry mouth, constipation, confusion.
A drug blocking histamine H1 receptors; sedating ones (hydroxyzine) are used as anxiolytics and sleep aids.
Blockade of dopamine D2 receptors — the core antipsychotic action, which also drives EPS and prolactin elevation.
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor — the first-line antidepressant class (e.g. sertraline, escitalopram).
Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor — antidepressants (venlafaxine, duloxetine) that also relieve some pain.
Monoamine oxidase inhibitor — a potent but demanding antidepressant class needing a low-tyramine diet and interaction caution.
Reversible inhibitor of MAO-A (moclobemide); tyramine can outcompete it, so no strict diet is needed and hypertensive risk is low.
An effective but cardiotoxic antidepressant class (wide QRS, arrhythmia, seizures in overdose) with anticholinergic effects.
A GABA-A-enhancing sedative-anxiolytic; effective short-term but risks dependence and lethal respiratory depression with opioids/alcohol.
Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (zopiclone, zolpidem) acting at the benzodiazepine GABA-A site, used short-term for insomnia.
An older sedative (phenobarbital) that prolongs GABA-A channel opening with no ceiling — high overdose lethality and enzyme induction.
Serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor (trazodone) — blocks 5-HT2A and, at higher doses, SERT, with sedating alpha-1/H1 blockade.
A 'typical' antipsychotic — strong D2 blockers with the most movement side effects (e.g. haloperidol).
An 'atypical' antipsychotic — generally fewer EPS but more metabolic (weight/glucose/lipid) risk than older agents.
A drug that treats psychosis mainly by blocking dopamine D2 receptors; split into first- and second-generation agents.
A chemical family of antipsychotics (chlorpromazine, fluphenazine); potency, not the family, predicts the side-effect pattern.
A high-potency antipsychotic family (haloperidol, droperidol) — strong D2 blockade, marked EPS, little sedation.
An antipsychotic family (e.g. zuclopenthixol) available in short-acting and depot injectable forms.
A drug (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) that boosts acetylcholine to treat Alzheimer dementia; GI cholinergic effects are common.
A drug that depletes presynaptic dopamine (e.g. tetrabenazine) — the mechanistic treatment for tardive dyskinesia.
A drug blocking the NMDA glutamate receptor; memantine uses this to dampen excitotoxic glutamate in dementia.
A drug that treats and prevents both poles of bipolar disorder (e.g. lithium, valproate, lamotrigine).
A seizure-suppressing drug; several (valproate, carbamazepine, lamotrigine) double as mood stabilizers.
A hypnotic that blocks wake-promoting orexin (e.g. lemborexant); non-dependence-forming and used for sleep onset and maintenance.
A drug (ramelteon) that promotes sleep via melatonin MT1/MT2 receptors and the circadian system, without dependence.
The count of infection-fighting neutrophils in blood; tracked on clozapine, with strict thresholds to hold or stop the drug.
A muscle enzyme; a markedly raised CK signals muscle breakdown, as in neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
A measure of blood-clotting time on warfarin; it shifts when enzyme inducers or inhibitors change warfarin levels.
The Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale; buprenorphine induction waits until it shows objective withdrawal (~8-12).
The clinical criteria used to diagnose serotonin syndrome (e.g. clonus, agitation, hyperthermia); there is no confirmatory lab test.
A list of medications potentially inappropriate in older adults (e.g. anticholinergics, benzodiazepines).
